cards.text: To change an inherited member's access specifier in a derived class, use: {{c1::using Base::memberName;}} under the new access specifier.

cards.question: What access level does printValue() have in Derived?
cards.explanation: The using declaration changes printValue from protected (in Base) to public in Derived.

cards.front: Can you change the access specifier of a private base member to public in a derived class?
cards.back: No, you can only change access specifiers for base members the derived class can access. Private members are inaccessible, so they can't be changed.

cards.text: To hide a public base class member in a derived class, use {{c1::using Base::member;}} under a {{c2::private:}} access specifier.

cards.question: Can the public access m_value through Derived?
cards.explanation: m_value is now private in Derived due to the using declaration, so the public cannot access it through a Derived object.

cards.front: Can encapsulation in a derived class be subverted by casting to a base class reference?
cards.back: Yes, if a member is public in Base but made private in Derived, you can still access it by casting to Base& and accessing it there.

cards.text: To prevent a function from being called through a derived object, mark it as: {{c1::= delete}}

cards.question: Will this compile?
cards.explanation: getValue() has been marked as deleted in Derived, so it cannot be called through a Derived object.

cards.front: After deleting a function in a derived class, can you still call the base class version?
cards.back: Yes, you can call Base::getValue() directly or upcast to Base& to access it.

cards.front: Can you change the access specifier for just one overload of a function?
cards.back: No, using Base::functionName affects ALL overloads with that name. You cannot selectively change access for individual overloads.

cards.concept: Ways to hide or restrict inherited functionality
cards.explanation:
Three approaches:
  • Change access specifier using 'using' declaration (e.g., make public member private)
  • Delete the function (= delete)
  • Use private inheritance (makes all inherited members private)
Note: Encapsulation can be bypassed by casting to Base&.
